story_title: Iran nuclear talks and moratorium proposal
event_summary: US and Iran signaled progress toward a nuclear deal featuring a 20-year moratorium on enrichment, with China acting as diplomatic intermediary; Trump publicly set a timeframe while Iranian officials framed enrichment as a sovereignty achievement.
tldr: With only 9 headlines across 8 buckets, the corpus is thin but cleanly bifurcated: Western-aligned outlets (Israel, Ukraine, Canada) frame the story around the moratorium's terms and Trump's timeline, while China, Pan-Arab, and Qatar headline the diplomatic coordination between Washington, Beijing, and Tehran. Russia's headline is the outlier, framing Iranian enrichment as a sovereignty victory rather than a concession — the only title that treats the program as an achievement rather than a problem to solve. The UK bucket's sole headline covers an unrelated Trump gaffe, signalling editorial deprioritisation of the nuclear track.

evidence.quote: Iran suspending its nuclear program for 20 years
Trump sets timeframe for moratorium on Iran's nuclear program
Chinese, Iranian diplomats meet before US-Iran nuclear talk
Trump, China's Xi 'agree Iran cannot have nuclear weapons'
Trump, Tehran signal talks as Lebanon truce extended

isolation_top.note: Headline angle on domestic Canadian reaction to the moratorium diverges from the diplomatic-process centre of the corpus.
UK headline covers Trump gaffe unrelated to nuclear talks — complete topic departure from the rest of the corpus.
Chinese-intermediary framing (diplomat meetings) sits orthogonal to Western moratorium-terms coverage.
Joint Trump-Xi headline bundles nuclear and Hormuz tracks, diverging from nuclear-only framing elsewhere.
Trump-timeline headline is semantically close to the Israel moratorium headline but adds urgency language absent elsewhere.

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Sole UK headline covered an unrelated Trump gaffe — nuclear track entirely absent from UK headline framing today.

bottom_line: A thin 9-headline corpus splits cleanly between moratorium-terms framing (Israel/Ukraine/Canada) and diplomatic-alignment framing (China/Pan-Arab/Qatar), with Russia's sovereignty-achievement headline as the sole counter-narrative. The UK's editorial choice to skip the nuclear headline entirely is the most notable absence.
